card.indications: Improvement of glycemic control in adults and children with diabetes mellitus.
card.mechanism: Replaces endogenous insulin, promoting cellular glucose uptake and inhibiting hepatic glucose production. Onset is rapid and duration is short.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized by carbohydrate intake, glucose level, insulin sensitivity, illness, and concurrent basal insulin. Subcutaneous doses are generally given immediately before a meal; product-specific instructions may allow dosing shortly after starting a meal.
card.routeAdministration: Verify insulin type, concentration, dose, glucose, and meal availability. Use only compatible pumps or IV protocols. Rotate injection sites. Do not share pens or needles.
card.contraindications: Episodes of hypoglycemia and serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Severe hypoglycemia can cause seizures or death. Medication errors, hypokalemia, fluid retention with thiazolidinediones, and pump failure can cause serious harm.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Hypoglycemia, injection-site reactions, lipodystrophy, weight gain, and edema.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypoglycemia, hypokalemia, anaphylaxis, and diabetic ketoacidosis from interrupted delivery.
card.interactions: Other glucose-lowering drugs increase hypoglycemia. Beta blockers may mask symptoms. Corticosteroids and sympathomimetics may raise glucose. Alcohol can unpredictably alter glucose.
card.monitoring: Monitor bedside glucose, A1C, meal timing, potassium during IV therapy, injection sites, and hypoglycemia frequency.
card.patientTeaching: Match the dose to meals exactly, carry fast-acting glucose, know sick-day rules, rotate sites, and verify the label before every injection.
card.nutrition: Coordinate closely with carbohydrate intake. Do not delay or skip the planned meal after dosing.

card.indications: Basal glycemic control in adults and children with diabetes mellitus, depending on product labeling.
card.mechanism: Forms a subcutaneous depot that releases insulin slowly, providing prolonged basal insulin activity with little pronounced peak.
card.dosing: Give once daily or according to the specific concentrated product schedule at the same time each day. Dose is individualized and not interchangeable unit for unit across all glargine products without prescriber direction.
card.routeAdministration: Subcutaneous use only. Do not mix or dilute with other insulins. Verify concentration carefully, rotate sites, and never share pens.
card.contraindications: Episodes of hypoglycemia and serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Severe hypoglycemia, medication errors between concentrations, hypokalemia, and fluid retention with thiazolidinediones can occur. It is not for treatment of diabetic ketoacidosis.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Hypoglycemia, injection-site reactions, lipodystrophy, weight gain, and edema.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypoglycemia, hypokalemia, anaphylaxis, and heart failure when combined with thiazolidinediones in susceptible patients.
card.interactions: Other antidiabetic drugs increase hypoglycemia. Beta blockers may mask symptoms. Corticosteroids, diuretics, and sympathomimetics may raise glucose. Alcohol alters glucose unpredictably.
card.monitoring: Monitor fasting and overall glucose trends, A1C, hypoglycemia, potassium when indicated, dose timing, and injection sites.
card.patientTeaching: Use at the same time daily, do not mix with other insulin, verify the concentration, carry glucose, and follow sick-day instructions.
card.nutrition: Maintain consistent meal timing and carbohydrate patterns even though this is basal insulin.

card.indications: Improvement of glycemic control in adults and children with diabetes mellitus.
card.mechanism: Rapidly replaces mealtime insulin, increasing peripheral glucose uptake and suppressing hepatic glucose output.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized by carbohydrate intake, current glucose, correction factor, activity, and concurrent basal insulin. Give within the product-specific window around meals.
card.routeAdministration: Verify product concentration and meal availability. Rotate sites. Use only approved pumps and IV protocols. Do not share pens or needles.
card.contraindications: Episodes of hypoglycemia and serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Severe hypoglycemia, medication errors, hypokalemia, pump interruption, and fluid retention with thiazolidinediones may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Hypoglycemia, injection-site reactions, lipodystrophy, weight gain, and edema.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypoglycemia, hypokalemia, anaphylaxis, and diabetic ketoacidosis from interrupted pump delivery.
card.interactions: Other glucose-lowering drugs increase hypoglycemia. Beta blockers may mask symptoms. Corticosteroids and sympathomimetics may raise glucose. Alcohol can increase or delay hypoglycemia.
card.monitoring: Monitor glucose before meals and as ordered, A1C, potassium during IV use, injection sites, and recurrent hypoglycemia.
card.patientTeaching: Time each dose with food, carry glucose, inspect the label and concentration before injecting, rotate sites, and know pump-failure and sick-day plans.
card.nutrition: Coordinate exactly with carbohydrate intake and avoid delaying the meal after dosing.

card.indications: Maintenance bronchodilation in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; nasal products reduce rhinorrhea from allergic or nonallergic rhinitis or the common cold.
card.mechanism: Blocks muscarinic receptors in airway or nasal tissues, reducing vagally mediated bronchoconstriction and secretions.
card.dosing: Inhaled dosing is product specific and often scheduled several times daily. Nasal dosing depends on concentration and indication. It is not the preferred sole rescue treatment for sudden severe bronchospasm.
card.routeAdministration: Teach correct inhaler, spacer, nebulizer, or nasal-spray technique. Avoid spraying into the eyes. Prime devices as directed and clean equipment regularly.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity to ipratropium, atropine derivatives, or product components.
card.warnings: Paradoxical bronchospasm, acute angle-closure glaucoma from eye exposure, urinary retention, and severe hypersensitivity can occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry mouth, cough, throat irritation, headache, bitter taste, nasal dryness, and nosebleed.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe bronchospasm, anaphylaxis, acute glaucoma, eye pain with vision changes, and urinary retention.
card.interactions: Other anticholinergic drugs can increase dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, and glaucoma risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor breath sounds, work of breathing, symptom relief, rescue use, eye symptoms, urinary retention, and technique.
card.patientTeaching: Use on schedule, keep prescribed rescue medication available, avoid eye exposure, and report worsening wheeze, eye pain, halos, or inability to urinate.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions. Maintain hydration for dry mouth unless fluids are restricted.

card.indications: Prevention of angina pectoris due to coronary artery disease. It is not intended to terminate an acute angina attack.
card.mechanism: Converted to nitric oxide, relaxing vascular smooth muscle. Venodilation reduces preload and myocardial oxygen demand; arterial dilation may improve coronary flow.
card.dosing: Immediate-release products are commonly given twice daily with doses separated to provide a nitrate-free interval. Extended-release products are usually taken once each morning. Dose is individualized.
card.routeAdministration: Check blood pressure and symptoms before administration. Swallow extended-release tablets whole unless the specific product allows splitting. Maintain the prescribed nitrate-free interval to reduce tolerance.
card.contraindications: Use with phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors such as sildenafil, tadalafil, or vardenafil; use with riociguat; severe hypotension; and serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Can cause profound hypotension, syncope, reflex tachycardia, and worsening angina if stopped abruptly. Tolerance develops with continuous exposure. Headache is common and expected early in therapy.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, dizziness, flushing, orthostatic hypotension, nausea, and weakness.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypotension, syncope, myocardial ischemia from excessive blood-pressure reduction, and methemoglobinemia.
card.interactions: PDE-5 inhibitors and riociguat are contraindicated. Alcohol, antihypertensives, and other vasodilators increase hypotension.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, heart rate, angina frequency, dizziness, adherence, and nitrate-free interval.
card.patientTeaching: Take on schedule for prevention, not acute pain. Keep prescribed sublingual nitroglycerin available for attacks. Rise slowly and never combine with erectile-dysfunction drugs.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food consistently. Avoid alcohol because it increases hypotension.
